commentBody:
My five favorite original clues from last week
(in order of appearance):

1. Slice of Life or nick of Time?  (5)(3)
2. They typically come in sets of four  (4)
3. "What on earth WILL you show some respect for?"  (3)(7)(6)
4. Overtook?  (7)
5. What might add a bit of flavor to a salsa?  (5)


PAPER CUT
PAWS
IS NOTHING SACRED
HOARDED
BONGO

"You can't remember how to play Scrabble?"
"I'm drawing a blank."

("Well, that's pointless.")

10 Items or FEWER. 

Just sayin’

Fine, straightforward Monday grid but a pretty bland theme today, in my opinion. For this to be interesting or amusing to me I’d like to see each entry reflect a slightly different take on the revealer; for these, you FEEL for them in all the same ways. Frankly I didn’t even see how SCRABBLETILE worked here until I read the column. Ok, I guess you feel for it in the bag when you pick it, but you don’t really “feel” for it like you do with a light switch or loose change when you grope around for it trying to find it; you just pull one out of the bag and they are right there. So, meh for me today; but hard to get worked up over a Monday.

By Monday standards pretty tough.  Having the inherently ambiguous EEG in there with a crossing like NEW ERA not everyone has heard of.  Then ATHEISM crosssing ASMR which people have never heard of when ATHEIST arguably fits the phrasing better.  Just confusing and ambiguous for a Monday.

This has never happened before.

I finished the puzzle with little effort.  It said I had a mistake, I went over and over and over it.

I finally got sick of looking and did "Reveal Puzzle".  It said the "M" in FARM was wrong, at the cross with MUSS.

I revealed the letter, and it said M.

Did no one else have this problem?

I’m not an expert on Mondays, but this seemed like a tougher Monday? I guess we’ll see.

I’ve always heard the expression, no fuss, no muss. Either I learned the expression wrong or people say a different ways.

I’m sad. The World Cup is over.  nothing compares to the excitement of the global game.  
Not to get to political, but did Trump have to hand out every medal.  I mean the leaders of Mexico and Canada didn’t get to do anything, and they cohosted this tournament. Of course, I’m not surprised by our president. but I would have thought FIFA would’ve been a little more equitable     Smh.
Well, it was a puzzle.

"10 ___ or less" (sign at checkout)"
If it is countable then it's "fewer", not "less".

If only I weren't so full of GLEE for Spain creaming Argentina in the World Cup final, I might make SELECCION ARGENTINA a theme entry in this puzzle... but alas (and please forgive my lack of compassion and the tinge of schadenfreude) I am not feeling even a bit for the losing team... Congrats to Spain for a beautiful game and well-deserved trophy! Congrats as well to Mr. Rollfing for yet another lovely romp, and thank you! NEWERA and ALGI were complete unknowns, and I had to wait a while for EEGs and DRIVER to come to me and resolve the white squares. That was a nice bit of spice for a Monday. Wishing all a wonderful week, I do something I haven't yet done – link to a full album. https://youtube.com/playlist?list=OLAK5uy_koE4rtsIJ002pzrNOUIjuhnft_Aun9pkY&si=DRwJv1dzRucPhER4 The album is Talk Talk's "The Colour of Spring" which contains the song "CHAMELEON Day." Aside from the puzzle having sparked the song in my mind, I link the full album because I'm a huge fan of Talk-Talk, consider Mark Hollis (RIP) a genius musician, and regret terribly that they were one of the most underrated bands of the 80s. This year it will have been 40 years since the album was published, and Hollis's fusion of jazz and art-pop remains timeless. This is my small way of honoring him.

That was quick but fun! I'm all caught up with my crosswords now, since I started my vacation on Saturday (I was a few days behind). I noticed that the Midi has started keeping track of streaks, previously my stars were all blue but now a bunch are gold.

I hardly ever carry cash anymore, it's nice not to have to fumble around for POCKET CHANGE. My purse feels a lot lighter too, lol

Count me as one who has their SCRABBLE TILEs in a Crown Royal bag! 😂

I think there was a typo! I had a tough time with the “Begin to grow choppers” because I think it was supposed to be “chompers”. I was only able to complete that clue with solving for the other letters.
Yay, España! (or Olé)

Inspired by the picture Sam's provided us today, here's the Rolling Stones and Florence Welch with a beautiful rendition of Wild Horses from some years ago. https://youtu.be/-8YRkL_pzQA?si=Egxu0Bum622h_tQe Aside to the constructor: There's an episode of the TV show The Closer that has storing a gun in a Crown Royal bag as a plot point.
had SCRABBLEwords for too long, then deleted it all, which is why that area took too long. still, a nice break from yesterday’s crossword.
I can produce some tingling sound samples regarding 31A.
Which complaints about cluing and entries constitute whining?  How easy is a Monday puzzle supposed to be?
Not a Monday puzzle in my opinion.

My brother is proudly dyslexic and i hope it’s not too politically incorrect to say that i invented something called dyslexic scrabble — you have to spell the words wrong. It’s great fun and quick and much less arduous than real scrabble. Difficult tiles are a breeze because you can use them to spell words like LANGWIJ or CHIQIN or BOOX. Or, for a confident spelur, ZAILAFOWN. Try it!

Prepare for a challenge if you accidentally spell something correctly!

PS A fun Monday puzzle today, i used only the Across clues and finished in 18:09. Yay!
I would have gotten your clue right away. Our Scrabble tiles are also stored in a Crown Royal bag!
Last night this one topped off a very enjoyable solving weekend.  Even did Sunday's, which I normally skip, but this time I rose to the occasion.  Many thanks to all.
If you'd like more of a challenge from a fresh puzzle, try: https://www.newyorker.com/puzzles-and-games-dept/crossword/2026/07/20

I had already uncovered the theme answers, and when I revealed I FEEL FOR YOU, then looked at LIGHT SWITCH, it was a big “Huh?”, and then came a big “Oh!”, when I realized, “Ah, when it’s dark!”  And I actually pictured feeling for a light switch in a dark room.

I made a similar leap when I went from picturing SCRABBLE TILEs on the game board to the ones in the bag.  And I pictured feeling for them in said bag; I even heard the sound made when the fingers jostle those in-bag tiles.

How often do puzzles draw in the imagination like that?   That made this puzzle a cut above for me.

Adding serendipitous spice were a trio of palindromes (ALA, EKE, PUP), a profusion of double E’s (9!), and a rare-in-crosswords five-letter semordnilap (STRAW).

A sweet experience all around, a lovely wake-up for the week ahead.  Thank you, Eric!

As someone from Hungary, today’s puzzle was so refreshing! It relied more on vocabulary than on American sports for example, so I barely had to guess with the autocheck feature. Really enjoyed it, thank you!

In the ice cream parlor I owned
with my hubby (who made the cream)
for twelve years,
we offered cakes.

Smallest size was a 6” round.

Decorating them was on me,
and I was quite proud of my prowess
with an icing bag and gel tubes.

One woman called and asked for
“Happy Birthday Victoria” 
to be written on it.

Tricky, because 
with piping around the edge
I was left with only about a 4” circle
as my canvas.

“And could you add horses?”

…?…

I saiid I’d *try* to squeeze in some 
horse head, somehow.

“But not tame.
She loves wild horses,
so the horse should look
like a horse running free on the beach.”

In - at most - a 1” square.
With frosting.

A wild-eyed
untethered, majestic animal
with a sea breeze flowing
through its billowing mane.

Some days, I miss our little microcreamery.

Some days, I do not.
Was on AUTOPILOT for most of this until it came time to crack the theme, which was pretty clever!  So this was a very good Monday puzzle!
Did you feel for the switched the light? https://youtu.be/ZdIJOE9jNcM?si=iYG6RQjYMGVfqAlj

Isn't IFEELFORYOU kind of a downer?

But IFEELYOU - that would have been something to work with.
Nitpicky, but I would have preferred calculation over error in 41a.

Solved this one using only the horizontal clues, which made figuring out the thematic entries and the revealer a lot more fun than usual. Last box filled was the G common to EEGS and ALIG, only because ALIG looked so utterly wrong to me. But EEGS seemed the only option, so in went the G. 

Found the theme rather weak, but it was compensated for by the interesting long vertical entries.

Mondays are a lot more fun now that I've started using just half the clues. So, while I have the time to spend, I think I'll continue solving Monday puzzles in this way.

47D? if only . . . Though the problem is not with the programmers, it's with the implementation. 6D, baseball fact I've never heard from Steve. Karma! earworm https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JmcA9LIIXWw

It's been a while since I really didn't get the theme at all even after solving the entire puzzle. I get it now, after reading this column, but still feel like it's kind of weak. Makes me think of some of the categories that have commenters in the Connections Companion page grumbling.

A couple of dead ends from trying to understand the theme: I FEEL FOR YOU for: having been selected for secondary screening; getting stuck with a Q/Z tile at the end of the game, or seeing me play such a tile on a triple letter square forming two words. But LIGHT SWITCH? Couldn't think of anything.

I hereby take issue with Sam's contention that "get-up-and-go" with hyphens necessarily denotes a noun.  The noun may just as well be written without hyphens.

Am I missing a Crosslandic convention here?

Good Monday puzzle - a bit on the slow side for me, but things fell together from working the crosses. And... must confess that I had to stop and ponder for a just a bit after I filled in the reveal to grasp how the theme worked. Still seems just a bit of a stretch, but I guess it works. And of course a puzzle find. A Wednesday from May 4, 2011 by Jeff Chen. Five down answers that ended in a square that was blocked in - so that letter was not in any across answers. Some examples: MADAMI (MADAMIMADAM) ABLEWASIER (ABLEWASIEREISAWELBA) ROTA DEIF AIBOHP Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=5/4/2011&h=25d ...
I’d say that I 33A that we’re living in a time of 40A.

FOR THE EDITORS; 
Can the app be modified so that GOLD is awarded to those that dont do “REVEAL” because the 24 hour clock dings those that complete puzzles without help days later.

And with that, I hit my first 365 day streak! For years I have been wanting to teach myself how to do a "proper crossword", and somewhere along the road, stumbled across the NYT crossword. As a Brit with a different approach to spelling (what's the colour of aluminium I hear you ask?!!), I definitely didn't make life easy for myself but regardless, started plodding away day by day. Only 18 months ago I managed to break free of the shackles of the Thursday, progressing to the Friday (with huge thanks to the Easy Mode newsletter for teaching the tricks of the trade!) and the tackle the impenetrable Saturday. 
Replacing the social media app so often clicked on my phone front page with the NYT Games app, doing puzzles instead of doomscrolling, the streak started in earnest. With a little assistance from my "partner-in-crime" wife still on the weekends (Sundays are her fav, she can't resist getting involved!) , the year flew past with the fun, discovery and challenge of puzzling. Thanks so much to all the constructors and the NYT puzzle team over the past 365 days for their hard work - it has been a wild ride, and long may it continue! Right, time to start delving the archives!

One more puzzle find - a Thursday from October 24, 2019 by Matt Ginsberg. All of the theme answer clues began with:
"When prefixed with 72-across...". Some of those theme answers: 

BUTHEBEST
TOSNEEZEAT
UPMYSLEEVE
LEFTBEHIND

And... the last across in that puzzle was 71-across, so there was no 72-across. Thought that was pretty clever.

....

Definitely need to get checked out...a miss on a Monday!  I see it is ASMR, which is equally opaque for me.
I asked Audiophile DHubby (at least 60 years of obsession) about ASMR and he's never heard of it.  
Some kind of reverb? Audio is in the clue, so A is for...??  
Help help
NADER crossing with NEW ERA was far too much American trivia for my British Rolodex of crossword knowledge.

Cute, unguessable revealer.

Given it's position in the grid I thought SPOUSES was a themer and it works, imo.

Late at night in bed when it's dark or in the morning when you don't want to open your eyes yet, one might feel for one's spouse.

Well, the first words of each themer had nothing in common and the second words of each themer had nothing in common so therefore all the answers as a whole had to have something in common.  So I tried to figure out what?  You flick it on?  Nah.  It sits on a rack?  Nah.  It jingles?  Nah. I could have stared at the puzzle until the cows came home and not figured out the common bond.

Even when I had IF...FORYOU, I didn't figure it out.  IF NOT FOR YOU?  I FELL FOR YOU?

And then...bingo!

The SCRABBLE answer must mean that the tiles you pick from are in a bag.  I think that when I played SCRABBLE w-a-a-a-y back in the day, we put the tiles face down on the table and picked carefully from the group -- as carefully as if we could actually see what they were.  What  a waste of time turning all those tiles upside down after each game.  A bag would have been so much better.

A cute and original idea for a puzzle theme --  boasting a nice junk-free grid.  Well, with the exception of ASMR.  You can tell me I've seen it before and I probably have -- but however many times I see it, I will never have the vaguest idea what it means, nor will I remember the clue.  That's a promise.

And one nit at 4D.  ATHEISM is far more likely to stem from never having had faith than from having had it once and now having it "no more."  The "no more" in the clue is cutesy but inaccurate.

Still -- I wish that all Mondays were as breezy, as junk-free and as enjoyable.

Chaka, Chaka, Chaka, Chaka Khan
Chaka Khan, Chaka Khan, Chaka Khan

(60A)

Slightly chewy Monday and I needed to stare at the revealer for a while before I got it, but nothing wrong with that.

Life settling back to normal now the World Cup has run its course. The best team won. The less said about antics post match the better.
Fun puzzle! And, set my new Monday PR at 3:20!  ZOOM!!! Hopefully spelling bee takes me a while today!
Another thing you might feel for: LOSTGLASSES

Oh. 
I thought the plural of "spouse" would obviously be "spice."

A walk in the (herb) garden, with RUE, SAGE, and I suppose STRAW. Ketch, Critter, and their buds invite you to a wild party: https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=yedrXTmtIfI&list=RDyedrXTmtIfI&start_radio=1&pp=ygUgb2xkIGNyb3cgbWVkaWNpbmUgc2hvdyBodW1kaW5nZXKgBwE%3D

Eric, your comment about a Crown Royal bag reminded me of finding same bag hidden in a drop ceiling of a rental I lived in 40 years ago.  Hoping to find $$, I was disappointed to find only a very old stash of Mary Jane.  [No, didn’t smoke it]
Oddly difficult today. I just wasn't on the right wavelength, I guess. Even a Monday can throw you a curve from time to time.

The SCRABBLE TILE theme entry reminded me that at age 5, shortly after being introduced to Scrabble by my grandparents, I developed an amazing ability to sense via braille which letter I was about to pluck out of the bag of tiles, and pick something else if it wasn't the letter I wanted. This was not unrelated to my grandfather's offer of a dollar if I ever got a seven-letter word.
Weird Al knows the difference: https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=8Gv0H-vPoDc&list=RD8Gv0H-vPoDc&start_radio=1&pp=ygUUd2VpcmQgYWwgd29yZCBjcmltZXOgBwE%3D (at 1:00 min. in)

Regarding questionable signage at the grocery store, I once saw a hand-lettered sign at mine proscribing "pandering," among other things. I asked the manager if he had an issue with prostitution in the parking lot. (It was a rough part of Columbus.) The sign was supposed to read "no panhandling."
Daily brain tests for short= Final Jeopardy.
ASMR is obscure. Probably not quite right for a Monday...

Breezy for me and I used only the down clues.  I had never tried that before.  Fun!  Though I almost missed my favorite clue of the day (40 Across Blue state?) but I had time  and inclination to go back and peruse.
Lost the gold star on Atheist/m. Bummer. Oh well. Cheers and Happy Monday!

poem made from words in today’s puzzle

a/ in my orchard of regret is a small hammock for the scarecrow — who had a brain — and i regret to say the scarecrow is d/ not thinking more on this
it’s a shame, really…
who’s to go and put back the
a/ wet straw
I really do think there were 2 correct solutions today with 4 down and 10 across both working with atheist/ASTR and atheism/ASMR,  the former being a techno pop group from NYC.

Rather amazing late puzzle find (might re-post this tomorrow). A Sunday from June 23, 2013 by Patrick Berry with the title: "Two-By-Fours." That one had four across theme answers and four down theme answers, and each of those contained four rebus squares of the same letters: Some examples (with the rebus squares in parentheses). F(OR)BETTER(OR)F(OR)W(OR)SE S(IN)G(IN)(IN)THERA(IN) W(ER)N(ER)KLEMP(ER)(ER) (TH)IS(TH)ATAND(TH)EO(TH)ER M(AR)G(AR)ETF(AR)R(AR) CA(ST)(ST)HEFIR(ST)(ST)ONE H(OW)N(OW)BR(OW)NC(OW) (ON)CEUP(ON)AH(ON)EYMO(ON) And of course the crossing non-theme answers had to included those rebuses also. https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=6/23/2013&h=45a ....
ASMR got me! Never heard of it. So it was atheism not atheist! Ouch!
It should be "10 items or fewer" but OK.

I learned several things today, which is super cool for a Monday! I struggled a bit with this one compared to other Mondays. I realized I’ve often entered the word “atoll” in crosswords (from filling out other clues) but never actually knew what it meant. No bathing suits here!

I'm shocked, shocked I say, that ASMR is coming up so frequently in these comments as an unfamiliar clue and answer. Familiarity with ASMR, including its saturation in internet content for many years as to become something of a meme, is something I've clearly taken for granted. It was one of the quickest and most plainly obvious clue and answer for me in this puzzle, which may be in part why I didn't suffer the ATHEIS(M/T) dilemma many others did. 

Is ASMR age-dependent, or have I just run in exceedingly esoteric circles of the internet? Surely 28 isn't exceptionally young to be enjoying crosswords in the year of our Lord 2026?
Chaka Khan is somewhere feeling sad that she wasn’t involved in this puzzle’s revealer.

My favorite encore clues from last week:

[Does as a group?]  (4)
[You're doing it every day]  (5)


DEER
AGING
On #3 of the original clues it should be -- (2)(7)(6).  I'm sorry for the goof and I hope it didn't cause anyone any trouble!

@Lewis

I think #1 should be in the running for clue of the year.

@Lewis 
I have to admit, I just got #5 after reading your comment... when I did the crossword I was stumped, lol

@Lewis

Overtook? for HOARDED was a cut above IMO.

I remember reading the clue referencing Time and Life and wondering if younger solvers would get the references.

Nice niche you've carved out for yourself here, congrats.

@Lewis
#1 was my favorite, too. Not many clues make me smile these days, but this one did!
@Lewis what do the #s in parentheses signify?

@Mark

Word lengths.

Mike,

Pointless but very useful.

(Triple Crossword Score)

@Mike

Did you have to rack your brain for that one? By the way, my mother always liked the blank because it always made it easier for her to find a good word since she was getting on a bit—she called it “an old wives’ tile”.

@SP
I was originally going to suggest that the blank was useful because if you had an O,N and E you could make either RENO or NOME, making it a tile of two cities—but of course proper nouns aren’t allowed.

@Mike
Don't say another word. You've got this one in the bag.  Anyway, there's a 50/50 chance you've still got all your marbles if you'd like to play something else.

@Mike
Well, there's no tile like the present tile to rack it up again.

@SP
We used to play Proper Name Scrabble in which /only/ prooer names were allowed. Ferocious arguments ensued, up to and including expeditions in search of sample packages from the kitchen or bathroom to prove that a disputed word was or was not part of a trade name.

@MJ

Grammatically, but that’s not usually how the sign reads.
@SP I know, and it annoys me there too. I guess I assumed people knew what I meant!

@MJ

I am really with you.  For some reason, misuse of "less" and "fewer" is something that really grates on my ear, and I have no idea why.

@MJ

In most other languages I’m aware of, this distinction is not made. In Spanish, for example, “menos” is used in either case; the same for “moins” in French. It’s a distinction that serves no purpose, so it’ll probably die out eventually.

@Francis,
Farther and further, as well.

@MJ

Apparently supermarket people couldn't care fewer.

@MJ

“I couldn’t care less” or is that “I could care less” 😉

@MJ

You can't expect the people in line to be sticklers for grammar when clearly they can't even count.
@SPDepends where you shop.  In our area, the grammar police have convinced the big chains (Wegmans, Topps) to use "fewer".

@SP

You're obviously a person with a strong ETHIC, but some folks—far less honest than you and I—might feel for the etched shapes in the bag.  ☺️

@HeathieJ

I wondered that but never tried it! Seems awfully hard to do and rather obvious to the other players if you did.

@SP

The naticky crossing of some Ioser politician, a brand of *baseball caps*, and an abbreviation of a medical test actually made me look up the brand 🤷🏽. The name of an American brand can be anything (I will never forget my shock at first seeing TCBY in these puzzles), and people's names often surprise me with their spelling, too (looking at you, Hoda Kotb). I didn't get the theme, either - I've never played Scrabble or carried change in my pocket (um, wallets?) - so I couldn't see the connection between the revealer and theme entries 🤷🏽

@HeathieJ

Okay, Helen Keller/Daredevil.

@HeathieJ

Not all Scrabble tiles have inscribed/etched letters. Some are just printed and have no surface variations.

@Andrzej

Ralph Nader deserves better that "some loser politician."

Poor Ralph Nader, remembered only as 'some loser politician'.
Seems to me the chief reason he kept losing was that he was not a politician.

@Andrzej

You may not even know the half of it on the "loser politician", Ralph Nader.  This will be old news to use older Americans, but you younger Europeans may not have followed along.

Nader's claim to fame was a book he wrote "Unsafe at all Speeds" about how American industry covered up hidden safety issues, in cars and airplanes, primarily, if I recall correctly.

Anyway, I don't think Nader ever held elected office, but he did have a solid following for many years.  And he got a handful of votes for President in many election cycles.  One of those years was 2000.

That year, the entire electoral college swung on the votes from Florida, and the voting there was incredibly close.  Ultimately the difference was about 700 votes in favor of Bush over Gore, out of nearly 6,000,000 cast.  Nader got nearly 100,000 votes in Florida.

Most people think Nader took more votes from Gore than from Bush.  Assertions like that are always open to challenge.

But in this case, that 700 vote difference decided the Presidency of the 2000 election.  

Believe it or not, that is only a fraction, less than half, of the problems with the 2000 election.

@JayTee

Over here people of any gender who still use cash carry it in their wallets, and those feature coin pockets. Of course, for years now all I've ever had to have with me to pay at 99% of places has been my phone.

@Don H

Maybe he does, but all I know about him is how he was clued today, and all that gave me was "Ioser politician" 🤷🏽

@Don H

All I know about him I learned from today's clue, and all that gave me was "loser politician" /shrug

@JayTee
My father would never have used a coin purse, for some reason he though of it as a very female thing. He really scoffed at European men who had coins in some sort of purse. I don't know why!

@Don H

The bots are to blame for my double posts. I posted the other after the first one had been withheld (spelling "Ioser" with a capital "i" rather than an actual L helped, but then a human must have released the original iteration of the comment).

@Jane Wheelaghan
First, the very purse-iness of the thing.
Second, the delicate gesture needed to manoeuvre a coin out of the purse  as opposed to the manly fist plunged into the pocket.
Third, the implied lack of wealth that leaves  you tweedling around with pennies as opposed to masculine dominance as the breadwinner with a fistful of cash.
Ah, the joy of semiotics!

@Chris

It was the clue “bikini and others” that I thought wasn’t Monday. I reflexively put ATOLLS on first pass, but thought I’ll probably have to erase that later.

Chris,
ATHEISM is the grammatically correct answer; ATHEISt would be incorrect.  
ASMR was here just a month ago, on a Monday, from the same constructor.
I've never heard of NEWERA (as clued), but EEG was a no brainer (as it were).

@Barry Ancona 
I was just going to write a comment about the reappearance of ASMR so soon and by the same constructor. This time I recognized what the clue wanted, and fortunately the crosses provided the correct order of the letters. Next month I might actually remember the order.

@Chris

EEG is a brain test. - electroencephalogram. 

ECG (aka EKG) is a heart test. - electrocardiogram

Others, if I remember what you said, were more general scans, I think

@Chris

You all do realize that NEWERA is two words?

@Vaer I think ASMR is part of the new crosswordese that I'm going to have to memorize eventually. I've never heard anyone mention it in the wild, but it does show up regularly in some of the more free-wheeling, youth-oriented crosswords by organizations like AVCX and Puzzmo.

@Chris
I’m ok with asmr but a brand name crossing with an acronym is clunky for a monday.

@Chris
How is EEG "inherently ambiguous"? The clue and answer were very straightforward.

ATHEISM fits the clue, but atheist does not. An atheist is one who lacks faith; ATHEISM is the lack of faith.

I think, when you say "people have never heard of" ASMR, you mean you have never heard of ASMR. It is a very popular genre of video on YouTube, and has been for years. Millions upon millions of people have heard of it.
@marigold  I had an echocardiogram last year and the word 'baseball' in the brand name clue is implicative of earned-run-average (ERA) 🤔.
@Katie Among the billions upon billions of people who don't YouTube.

@Katie  ATHEISM is grammatically correct for the clue. But the word faith doesn't necessarily require belief in a god or gods but theism does. So lack of faith could imply atheism but atheism does not necessarily imply lack of faith.

I think I found the problem.  My keyboard has a key which shifts from US letters to Greek letters.

I must have accidentally had the "Greek" selected when I put in the "M", which looks identical to the US "M".

That's one for the record books.  

It's alarming enough to try to deal with 0 and O, now I have M (US) and Μ(Greek).

@Francis

It’s bananas that you actually discovered the reason. You can easily test out your theory on Tuesday puzzle

@Weak

Yeah, I already proved it to myself.  Here's the proof.

Search for "M" in this sentence.  "The Mouse climbed up the Μountain."

Only one "M" will be found.

@Francis

Wow that is an odd issue. I have the Greek keyboard as well but that’s never happened to me

@Francis: one time, wanting to include a Greek letter in a comment, I had my partner read out the instructions for adding another keyboard. Poof! EVERYTHING was now in Greek, names of apps, search results, everything. Took some ingenuity to read Greek instructions for undoing what I did. I guess there’s a right way to just add a keyboard, but I’m scared now. And your story proves I am right to be scared.

@Francis 
Did you forget about "climbed" or are the other two both Greek?

@SP

For me, on my Mac, the toggle switch is the lower left, a key which has a "globe" picture on it.  When clicked, it flips from US to Greek or Greek to US.  

I must have unknowingly hit that, typed the M, and then, also unknowingly, hit it again so that I didn't see any of the recognizable Greek symbols appear when I typed other letters.

This is a really, really nasty sequence of events, and I'm not entirely sure how to prevent it in the future.

@wlieu

At the risk of getting on the wrong side of any strict grammarians, I have to say that a language is a living, breathing thing. Making it go back where it came from is not easy to accomplish.

My personal pet peeves are:
1) when people say "stunning" for something yucky, like a "stunning pile-up on a highway in the snow" or even "a stunning volcano erupting"
2) when people pronounce "blender drink" like "blinder drink" and "Wednesday" like "Windsday"
3) when people say "sthring" for "string" and "sthreet" for "street"
 
But these changes--just like "10 items or less"-- are probably inevitable and trying to undo them is as impossible as making a puzzle that everyone will like!

@wlieu about 25 years ago the Co-oP in Berkeley changed their signs to “fewer”, and it sparked a change all over the east bay, at least. When I saw this clue was surprised the NYTimes would help enforce this bad grammar since the correct grammar in this widely known phrase helps figure out in other situations what the proper wording should be.

@RonH

Go Bears!

@RonH I Alas, the Coop is long gone, at least the grocery stores are. I favor ignoring these common nit in others but being more careful myself, especially in written text.

As long as we are being picky, this is a diction (word choice) question, not a grammar question. Diction questions tend to be less set in stone than grammar issues.

@lucky13
I was surprised by your peeves. I'm an open prescriptivist and think some changes should be opposed as contributing to the deterioration of language. But your list was outside my experience:
1. 'Stunning' only became viewed as favourable in US usage. It began by referring to an overwhelming or shocking event – i.e., something that left you stunned.
2 & 3. Mispronunciations you report are unusual. Perhaps they're regionalisms?

@Mean Old Lady

ASMR stands for Autonomous Sensory Meridian Response. It describes a pleasant, static-like tingling sensation that typically begins on the scalp or neck and moves down the spine. Often triggered by specific soft sounds or visual cues, it is frequently used to promote deep relaxation, reduce stress, and aid in sleep

@Mean Old Lady it stands for Autonomous Sensory Meridian Response, and it’s the tingling, goosebump inducing sensation received from certain types of audio (this can be anything from whispering to eating pickles). There are content creators and YouTubers whose whole channels are dedicated to this producing this kind of audio for their followers.

You nailed it - it's very likely a band reference. There probably aren't many hard rock fans here who would think of that. The constructor looks like a young man.

@Nancy While the others go down the rabbit hole on the ATHEISM clue, I'd like to share that ASMR stands for Autonomous Sensory Meridian Response, and refers to the tingling sensations from the scalp to the neck and down the spine that are triggered by various stimuli, such as soft touch and oddly satisfying sounds. Common cultural use of the term most often refers specifically to audio stimulus that triggers the response, as opposed to the response itself (e.g. "ASMR videos" of soft speaking or crinkling paper). Perhaps familiarity with it is strongly correlated with age (I'm on the younger side of 28), but I actually found that cloud and answer to be one of the quickest and most plainly obvious of this puzzle. To each their own strengths!
